summary: Criteria a handoff or review packet should satisfy in this repo. Calibrated against the PR #1 / #2 / #3 / #4 review packets that GPT Pro accepted, and against the prior-session handoff that hallucinated context (see anti_examples/wrong_repo_hallucination.md).

criteria.title: Repo identity is the first claim
criteria.rationale: A handoff that does not name the repo invites the next agent to import context from a different repo. The packet must state explicitly: 'This is the frontier-os repo, do not import context from ai-os, Siri, or companion-platform.' The marker shows up at the top, not buried.

criteria.title: Working tree state is reported, not assumed
criteria.rationale: Dirty trees are surfaced verbatim with the porcelain output. A clean working tree is asserted with evidence. Assumptions like 'main is coherent' are not acceptable substitutes for `git status --short`.

criteria.title: Verification commands and exact outputs are present
criteria.rationale: Claims like '17 tests pass' need the exact command and the exact pass-count output, not a paraphrase. A reviewer must be able to re-run any claim. PR review packets in this repo template this with explicit `$ <command>` blocks.

criteria.title: Risk notes call out non-obvious side effects
criteria.rationale: Anything the agent wrote outside the obviously-touched files, any process running on the system, any external surface inspected — name it. Example items: 'no external files in /Users/test/bin touched', 'ledger row count unchanged before/after', 'kill-switch state directory empty'.

criteria.title: Decisions a reviewer must accept-or-reject are listed explicitly
criteria.rationale: Format: 'Accept these N decisions to merge: (a) ..., (b) ..., (c) ...; reject any of them and a fixup is needed.' The reviewer should not have to infer what the explicit choices were. Past examples: JSON-vs-YAML spec format, read-only repair design, ledger-event-kind reuse.

criteria.title: Remaining ambiguity has its own section
criteria.rationale: Things that aren't done, things that are deliberate trade-offs, things that need follow-up. Naming them prevents the next agent from re-litigating them blindly. Phase 4's commit message dogfooding and the no-subprocess-timeout follow-up are recurring examples.

criteria.title: The packet states what was NOT done
criteria.rationale: Out-of-scope items are listed by name. 'Not in this PR: server-side hook, CI lint, prepare-commit-msg helper, Phase 5 taste rubrics.' This protects scope and shows the agent understood the boundary.

criteria.title: Diffs are full and referenced, not summarized
criteria.rationale: The packet includes the unified diff (or a path to it) and a stat line. Reviewers can ask for any specific file's full contents from the packet without needing repo access. Critical for private repos where reviewers cannot pull.

criteria.title: Anti-examples are encoded, not just narrated
criteria.rationale: When a previous PR was rejected for a specific failure mode, the eval suite or test suite must include a case that catches that mode. The handoff packet cites it. Examples in this repo: PR #1 v1's wrong-layer bug → eval criterion C8; PR #2 v1's narrow alert filter → eval criterion C9 + assertLegacyAndFactoryCoverage helper.

criteria.title: Length is calibrated to scope, not to look thorough
criteria.rationale: Small PRs get small packets. The Phase 1 packet was ~95KB because PR #1 had 1000+ LOC of new code; the Phase 4 packet was ~38KB because PR #4 had 470 LOC. Padding a small PR's packet to look comprehensive wastes reviewer time and obscures real signal.

non_goals: Marketing prose: handoffs are not announcements.
Speculation about future PRs: the packet covers THIS PR's state.
Self-congratulation: pass-counts and exact outputs are evidence; adjectives are not.
Hidden bypasses: 'I just merged because it looked OK' is the failure mode the rubric exists to prevent.

exemplars: PR #3 v2 (eval suite) — accepted by GPT Pro and merged at commit 25bdb31; the suite + tests live at evals/factory-quality/
PR #4 v2 (commit-msg guard) — accepted by GPT Pro and merged at commit d817267; the hook + tests live at scripts/hooks/ and tests/hooks/
